tldr: Across outlets on four continents, World Cup headlines split into two primary layers: a celebratory layer announcing Spain's triumph and Madrid's mass parade, and a governance-conflict layer driven by FIFA's investigation into Argentina's post-match brawl. A third politically charged layer appears in select outlets — Canada, Russia, India, and Vietnam leading with Trump's uninvited trophy-ceremony presence, while Iranian state media and The Guardian recast Spain's win as a statement of Palestinian solidarity.

silences.note: Daily Maverick's headline 'IN PICTURES: Tension and emotions at the 2026 FIFA World Cup' is an aggregate pictorial label; no specific frame signal is detectable from the headline alone.

Both Deutsche Welle headlines — 'Spain wins second World Cup after beating Argentina in final' and 'Taking stock of 2026 World Cup: Football's biggest show yet' — are a factual result announcement and a generic retrospective; neither carries a specific frame signal at headline level.

Yle's headline '2.1 million people watched Yle's coverage of the World Cup final' foregrounds a domestic viewership metric; the framing is self-referential and carries no issue-frame signal detectable from the title alone.

single_outlet_findings.finding: Only headline in the corpus to use 'farce' to describe Trump's trophy-ceremony appearance, the most overtly negative editorial word applied to that moment at headline level.
Only headline to name Netanyahu ('Bibi') alongside Trump as stakeholders disappointed by the result, explicitly fusing the US-Israel political axis into a sporting outcome at the title level.
Only headline to adopt a critical stance toward Argentina ('I Won't Cry for You, Argentina') rather than the sympathetic or neutral framing every other outlet used for Argentina's defeat.
Only outlet to make its own domestic audience size (2.1 million viewers) the headline angle, substituting a self-referential viewership metric for any framing of the match result.
Only headline to flag 混乱も ('some disorder/chaos') alongside the parade celebrations, surfacing disruption as a headline signal that no English-language outlet included in its title.

bottom_line: Headline coverage worldwide defaulted to celebration (Spain's Madrid parade) and governance (FIFA brawl probe), but sharply divergent political headlines — Trump-as-intruder in Western outlets, Trump-denying-Israel's-ally in Tehran, Gaza-as-proxy-ally in UK and Southeast Asian press — reveal editors selecting which dimension of the same event to lead with based on their outlet's political identity.
